## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Verdict

The revision improves several presentation points and fixes the LaTeX-contract issues, but the proof is still **not answer-ready**. The main remaining problems are not cosmetic: several essential technical lemmas are still asserted with insufficient proof, especially the coefficient-boundary identification for \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\), the direct-sum/finite-fusion coefficient calculus, and the passage from coefficient products to normal-bidual support inequalities.

### LaTeX-contract check

I reconstructed the revised `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. It compiled successfully. The resulting PDF has **6 pages**. I found no prohibited margin, line-spacing, or in-document font-size changes. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`. Thus the LaTeX contract is satisfied.

### Literature checks

- DKEP Section 2.3 does contain the one-normal \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\) support construction for normal \(M\)-\(C^*\)-algebras, including the support formula for invariant subalgebras; DKEP Proposition 2.10 records the simultaneous-normal version as an inclusion/equality statement in \(M(A)^{**}\). This supports part of the revised support-bookkeeping discussion, though the proof still packages several consequences too quickly. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) using all operators in \(e_BB(L^2M)e_B\cong B(L^2B)\), and Example 5.11 identifies the coefficient boundary for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence with that subalgebra boundary. The revised proof still needs an additional argument to pass from that \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence to the fused \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.10 has explicit hypotheses \(M,JMJ\subset M(X)\), \(N,JNJ\subset M(Y)\), and a cyclicity condition for vectors whose \(T_\xi\)-operators lie in \(YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\). The revised direct-sum lemma cites this theorem but still does not verify the needed cyclicity/closure details for arbitrary direct sums. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN does provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used by the draft, and Theorem 2.4 gives diffuseness/fullness/factoriality for join-irreducible graph products with state-zero unitaries. These final graph-product citations are substantially improved. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))
- DKEP Lemma 8.5 does support the normal-bidual central-state criterion and mentions the natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. The finite-boundary lemma is plausible, conditional on the earlier support-intersection lemma. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

## Section-by-section audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This section is acceptable. The draft explicitly adopts the standard interpretation of irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This addresses the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. Support bookkeeping

This section is improved relative to the previous version. The one-normal support formula is now closer to DKEP Section 2.3, and the simultaneous-normal version is at least plausibly obtained through Proposition 2.10.

However, the proof still compresses a nontrivial construction into a citation. In particular, the statement

\[
(Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\mathcal A_N(pr_Y)
\]

requires checking that the ordinary open support \(r_Y\) in \(A^{**}\) is the same support appearing under the simultaneous normal representation, and that the two normal structures interact exactly as claimed. DKEP gives the one-normal formula and then states a simultaneous inclusion result, but the equality in this exact corner form is not written out in the draft. This may be repairable, but in a self-contained proof it remains an essential lemma rather than a transparent citation.

The last assertion, \(q_X=pr_X\) for a boundary piece \(X\), is also too brief. The proof says DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Proposition 2.4 imply that functionals vanishing on \(X\) are precisely those vanishing on \(K_X(N)\). This is plausible, but the draft does not verify all hypotheses needed to conclude that \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is the **smallest** simultaneous strong bimodule generated by \(X\), nor does it spell out why this gives equality of the normal support projections of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\). Since the later intersection lemma depends directly on \(q_U=pr_U\), this is still an essential point.

### 3. Subalgebra coefficient boundary

The revision fixes one previous problem: it no longer relies on Hilbert-space rank-one operators generating all of \(B(L^2B)\). It correctly notes that DKEP Example 3.4 allows arbitrary \(S\in B(L^2B)\).

But the proof still does not establish the full lemma as stated. It verifies the formula

\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
=
T_\xi^*(T_{\bar\alpha}^{*}T_{\bar\beta})T_\eta
\]

for bounded elementary vectors and concludes that “all coefficients” of \(\mathcal H_B\) lie in \(X_B\). This is a gap. The coefficient boundary of a correspondence is generated by coefficients of **all bounded vectors**, not only elementary bounded tensors. To pass from elementary tensors to arbitrary bounded vectors, one needs a density/closure argument with uniform boundedness of the associated \(T_\xi\)-operators, or an application of DKEP Theorem 5.10. The draft does not provide this.

This matters because the later proof uses this lemma to identify \(X_{M_U}\) with the coefficient boundary of \(L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), and then constructs approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) from finite-fusion coefficients. Without the full coefficient-boundary identification, the main support-intersection argument is not established.

### 4. Direct sums and finite fusions

The revision adds the missing multiplier hypothesis \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\). That addresses one prior objection.

However, the proof remains too compressed at a crucial point. It says that for a direct sum, finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic dense and then “applying Theorem 5.10 once more” gives the conclusion for all bounded vectors. DKEP Theorem 5.10 requires a cyclic set of bounded vectors satisfying a specific \(T_\xi\in YB(L^2M,L^2N)X\) condition, not merely that the summands are individually relatively compact. The draft does not verify the required cyclic set for the direct sum, nor the closure/uniform boundedness details needed to pass from finite-support vectors to arbitrary bounded vectors.

The finite-fusion coefficient algebra \(\mathcal C_U\) is also still asserted rather than proved. The statement that finite products of coefficients are finite sums of coefficients after inserting conjugate factors is standard, but in this context it is an essential lemma because it feeds directly into \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\). The draft should at least give a precise formula with conventions and explain how finite sums/direct sums are handled.

### 5. Boundary-support intersection lemma

This is the central technical lemma, and it still depends on unresolved inputs.

The CDHJKN fusion rule itself is valid and appropriately cited. The rule decomposes fusions over \(M_{V_2}\) as sums indexed by subsets of the intersection of the relevant labels. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is the operator-algebraic passage from coefficient products to support inequalities. The line

\[
hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)
\]

rests on the direct-sum/finite-fusion lemma and on the coefficient-boundary identification above. Since those are not rigorously proved, this step is not secure.

The limiting argument in the ordinary bidual is clearer than before, and the use of strong-* convergence of approximate units is a reasonable route. But it still assumes the unproved support formula \(q_U=pr_U\) for the original boundary \(X_{M_U}\), and it assumes the existence of approximate units of \(X_{M_U}\) lying in the specified norm-closed coefficient cones. Both assumptions trace back to earlier underproved lemmas.

Thus the claimed inequality

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]

is still not rigorously established.

### 6. Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This lemma is mostly plausible. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) is well motivated, and DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the normal-bidual central-state criterion. The use of the compact-corner u.c.p. map \(\Theta_0\) is also consistent with DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The main caveat is that this lemma depends on the preceding palindromic product being genuinely in \(q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), which in this proof is supplied only by the still-unproved intersection lemma.

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This section remains plausible but not fully rigorous.

The AFP reduced-word commutator formula

\[
[p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B)
\]

is asserted after a brief explanation. A strict proof should check it on the vacuum, one-letter words, and longer reduced words. Similarly, the orthogonality claim

\[
q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0
\]

from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\) needs a complete reduced-word verification.

These are likely repairable gaps, not obviously false statements. But as written, the proof invokes several reduced-word facts without enough detail for a standalone rigorous solution.

### 8. Graph-product application

This part is much improved. The graph-product AFP decomposition is now cited, the graph combinatorics are correct, and the appeal to CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 is appropriate. I also ran a small brute-force check of the graph combinatorics for graphs up to seven vertices; no counterexample was found to the local claims used in choosing \(w_1,w_2\).

The final nonamenability argument is acceptable: CDHJKN gives a diffuse full factor under the stated hypotheses, and a separable diffuse amenable finite factor would be the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor, which is not full. The CDHJKN full-factor statement is explicitly supported by Theorem 2.4. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But the final application depends essentially on the relative Bass–Serre proposition, the coefficient-boundary lemmas, and the boundary-support intersection lemma. Since those are not fully proved, the final conclusion is not established.

## Summary of previous concerns

**Addressed or partly addressed:**
- LaTeX-contract compliance: fixed.
- Graph-product AFP citation: added.
- Nonamenability/factoriality citation: improved.
- Rank-one issue in the subalgebra coefficient argument: partly fixed by referring to arbitrary \(S\in B(L^2B)\).
- Missing multiplier hypotheses in Theorem 5.10 applications: partly fixed.

**Still remaining:**
- Full proof that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have identical simultaneous normal supports.
- Full proof that the fused correspondence \(L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary exactly \(X_B\).
- Direct-sum closure and finite-fusion coefficient calculus.
- The central support-intersection inequality \(q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}\).
- Detailed AFP reduced-word verification in the Bass–Serre proposition.

**New issue introduced or made clearer:**
- The direct-sum lemma now invokes DKEP Theorem 5.10 “once more” but does not verify the exact cyclic-vector hypothesis required by that theorem.

## Final assessment

The revised proof has a coherent strategy and may be repairable, but it still relies on several unproved essential technical lemmas. Therefore `answer.tex` does not yet fully solve the problem as a complete rigorous solution.

# Problem statement

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

# Author's solution attempt

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cF}{\mathcal F}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible is interpreted in the standard graph-product sense: \(\Gamma\) is
not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement graph \(\Gamma^c\) is
connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For \(U\subset V\),
\(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical expectation.

We use Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson's normal-bidual notation
\cite{DKEP}.  If \(N\) is finite, set \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^* ,
\]
the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual.  If \(X\) is an
\(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\) denotes DKEP's compact algebra associated
with \(X\), and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support projection of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For the compact boundary
\(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Put
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for every }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the non-existence of an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is the trace.

\section*{Normal supports and coefficients}

Let \(A=A_N\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 realizes
\(\cA_N=pA^{**}p\), where \(p\) is the simultaneous normal support projection
for the two commuting actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\).  If \(Y\subset A\) is a
hereditary C\(^*\)-subalgebra for which \(N\) and \(JNJ\) act faithfully by
multipliers, let \(r_Y\in A^{**}\) be its ordinary open support projection.

\begin{lemma}[normal support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
For such \(Y\), \(r_Y\) commutes with \(p\),
\[
       (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=(pr_Y)\,\cA_N\,(pr_Y),
\]
and the unit of this corner is \(s(Y)=pr_Y\).  If \((e_\lambda)\) is a
contractive approximate unit of \(Y\), then \(pe_\lambda p\to s(Y)\) strongly*
in \(\cA_N\).  Moreover, for \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\), the normal extension
\(\widetilde\phi\in(\cA_N)_*\) vanishes on \(s(Y)\cA_Ns(Y)\) if and only if
\(\phi|_Y=0\).

If \(X\) is a boundary piece with \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\) (in particular, a
subalgebra boundary \(X_B\)), then \(s(X)=s(K_X(N))\); hence \(q_X=s(X)\).
For any boundary piece \(X\), whenever \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\),
\[
       \phi|_X=0,\ \phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
       \widetilde\phi(T)=0                                             \tag{2.1}
\]
in the normal-bidual sense.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The first paragraph is precisely the concrete form of DKEP Proposition 2.10:
applied to \(Y\subset A\), it identifies \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) with the corner
\(r_YpA^{**}pr_Y\).  Since \(r_Yp=pr_Y\), this is
\((pr_Y)\cA_N(pr_Y)\).  A contractive approximate unit of \(Y\) converges
strongly* to \(r_Y\) in \(A^{**}\), hence its image \(pe_\lambda p\) converges
strongly* to \(pr_Y\).  The annihilator assertion follows because \(pYp\) is
weak* dense in that corner and the canonical pairing is
\(\phi(y)=\widetilde\phi(pyp)\).

For a boundary piece \(X\), DKEP Proposition 3.6 and Corollary 3.2 show that
\(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is contained in the simultaneous strong
\(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule generated by \(X\); Proposition 2.4 is the
corresponding annihilator criterion.  Hence every \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\)
that vanishes on \(X\) vanishes on \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), giving (2.1), and in
particular vanishes on \(K_X(N)\).  The converse is immediate from
\(X\subset K_X(N)\).  When \(N\) and \(JNJ\) act by multipliers on \(X\), the
normal corners associated with \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) therefore have the same
preannihilator, so their support projections agree.
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
In the tracial case we identify \({}_BL^2N_N\) with the conjugate
\(\overline{{}_NL^2N_B}\) through the Tomita conjugation.

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra coefficients]\label{lem:coeff}
The DKEP coefficient boundary of \(\cH_B\) is exactly the subalgebra boundary
\(X_B\) of Example 3.4 in \cite{DKEP}.  Also
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\) as an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\).  DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the left coefficient
boundary of \(H\) with \(X_B\).  For elementary bounded vectors in
\(H\otimes_B\overline H\),
\[
   T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{\,*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
        =T_\xi^{\,*}\,\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\,T_\eta,                 \tag{2.2}
\]
where \(\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\in \mathbb B(L^2B)\) is the rank-one coefficient
coming from the right \(B\)-module \(H\).  Formula (2.2) is the usual
linking-algebra form of \(T_{\xi\otimes\zeta}=T_\zeta T_\xi\).  DKEP
Example 3.4 realizes \(X_B\) as the hereditary algebra generated by the
operators \(T_\xi^*\,\mathbb B(L^2B)\,T_\eta\); hence all coefficients of
\(\cH_B\) lie in \(X_B\).  Conversely, taking
\(\alpha=\beta=\widehat1\) recovers the generators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of
\(X_B\).  Finally,
\(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes_BJ\xi\) is a well-defined
bimodular unitary, using traciality.
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\); hence \(X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_U}\).

\begin{lemma}[direct sums and finite fusions]\label{lem:dsupport}
Let \(X\subset A_N\) be a boundary piece.  If
\(\cK=\bigoplus_{j\in J}\cK_j\) is a direct sum of \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences
and the coefficient boundary \(X_{\cK_j}\) is contained in \(X\) for every
\(j\), then every bounded coefficient \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of \(\cK\) belongs to
\(K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), let \(\cF_U\) be the class of non-empty finite Connes
fusions of copies of
\[
        \cH_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M
\]
and of its conjugate.  The linear span \(\mathcal C_U\) of coefficients of
finite direct sums of members of \(\cF_U\) is a self-adjoint algebra, and
\(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit in the norm-closure of positive
coefficients \(T_\zeta^*T_\zeta\) from such finite direct sums.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Theorem 5.10, applied with the full boundary on the left and \(X\) on the
right, says that every bounded vector in each \(\cK_j\) gives a mixing operator
\(T_\xi\in K_{A_N,X}(N,N)\).  Finite direct sums are handled by finite row
matrices of such operators.  For an infinite direct sum, if \(P_F\) is the
projection onto a finite sub-sum, then \(P_F\xi\to\xi\) and
\(T_{P_F\xi}\to T_\xi\) in the strong module topology; the mixing operators are
strongly closed in Theorem 5.10.  DKEP Proposition 5.9 gives
\(T_\xi^*T_\xi\in K_X(N)\), and polarization gives all off-diagonal
coefficients.

The standard coefficient calculus for Connes fusion says that the product of two
bounded coefficients is again a bounded coefficient after adjoining the
correspondences and their conjugates as extra fusion factors; adjoints are
coefficients of conjugate correspondences.  Thus \(\mathcal C_U\) is a
\(*\)-algebra.  By Lemma \ref{lem:coeff}, \(X_{M_U}\) is the hereditary algebra
generated by positive
coefficients of \(\cH_U\).  If \(s=\sum_iT_{\xi_i}^*T_{\xi_i}\) is a finite sum
of such generators, the functions \(g_\varepsilon(t)=t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\)
form a contractive approximate unit for the hereditary algebra generated by
\(s\).  Polynomial approximation to \(g_\varepsilon^{1/2}\), with zero constant
term, writes \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) as a norm limit of \(c^*c\)'s with
\(c\in\mathcal C_U\), hence as a norm limit of positive coefficients from
finite direct sums of finite fusions.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .                                  \tag{3.1}
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^mU_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For \(R\subset V\), write \(H_R=L^2M\otimes_{M_R}L^2M\).  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4
identifies this with their graph-product correspondence, and Proposition 5.5
gives the fusion rule
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3.2}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction shows that any finite fusion with
labels \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes as a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).

Take positive coefficients \(h\) from finite direct sums of \(\cF_W\) and \(k\)
from finite direct sums of \(\cF_U\).  By the coefficient calculus just used in
Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport}, \(hkh\) is a finite sum of coefficients of finite
fusions whose labels are all \(W\)'s and \(U\)'s.  By the preceding label
calculus, each such fusion decomposes into a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset I\).  Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport}, and \(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\),
imply \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  The same holds for norm limits of such
positive coefficients.

Now the support computation is made in the ordinary bidual, where multiplication
is the usual one.  Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2M)\), let \(p\in A^{**}\) be DKEP's
simultaneous normal support, and let \(r_U\in A^{**}\) be the ordinary support
of \(X_{M_U}\).  By Lemma \ref{lem:support}, \(q_U=pr_U\), and each \(r_U\)
commutes with \(p\).  Choose the contractive approximate units
\(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and \(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\) supplied by Lemma
\ref{lem:dsupport}.  In \(A^{**}\),
\(h_\alpha\to r_W\) and \(k_\beta\to r_U\) strongly*.  Since
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), passing first in \(\alpha\)
and then in \(\beta\) gives
\[
        r_Wr_Ur_W\in r_{K_I}A^{**}r_{K_I},
\]
where \(r_{K_I}\) is the ordinary support of \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  This element
is a positive contraction, hence \(0\le r_Wr_Ur_W\le r_{K_I}\).  Compressing by
\(p\) and using the commutation with \(p\),
\[
        q_Wq_Uq_W
        =pr_W\,pr_U\,pr_W
        =p(r_Wr_Ur_W)p
        \le pr_{K_I}p=q_I,
\]
the last equality again being Lemma \ref{lem:support}.  This proves (3.1).
The palindromic statement follows by induction: if the product for
\(U_1,\ldots,U_{m-1}\) is a positive contraction dominated by \(q_J\),
\(J=\cap_{i<m}U_i\), then after compression by \(q_{U_m}\) it is dominated by
\(q_{U_m}q_Jq_{U_m}\le q_{J\cap U_m}\).
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{4.1}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to every \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  By DKEP Lemma 8.5, there is an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\), set \(q=q_i\).  Since
\(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\), for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
so \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\).  If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), then
\(y\mapsto\omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  On \(N\) it is a positive central functional
dominated by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\), hence normal; since \(N\) is a
factor, its normalized restriction is \(\tau\).  This contradicts relative
proper proximality, so \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for every \(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state on the ambient C\(^*\)-algebra \(\cA_N\).  In its
GNS representation, each \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, hence \(\omega(R)=1\).
The element \(R\) is a positive contraction and, by (4.1), \(0\le R\le q_0\);
therefore \(\omega(q_0)=1\).  DKEP Lemma 8.5 uses the canonical \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\longrightarrow
        (\mathbb K(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*=q_0\cA_Nq_0
\]
with \(\Theta_0(1)=q_0\) and \(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\) for \(x\in N\).  Thus
\(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is an \(N\)-central state on
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\), and \(\psi(x)=\tau(x)\) for \(x\in N\).  This is a
Connes hypertrace, contradicting non-amenability of \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal
form is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{n\ge1,\ i_1\ne\cdots\ne i_n}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n},\quad i_j\in\{P,Q\}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) be the projections onto the summands whose first letter is
\(P\), respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  If \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first letter except for the vacuum and
one-letter \(P\)-words, and
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJ e_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{5.1}
\]
The two terms in (5.1) are in the \(N,JNJ\)-bimodule generated by \(e_B\), so
they lie in \(X_B\).  For \(x\in Q\ominus B\) the same statement holds with
\(p\) and \(q\) interchanged, while \(B\) commutes with \(p,q\).  Since these
subspaces generate \(N\), DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) with normal
restriction to \(N\).  Left multiplication by \(w_i\in P\ominus B\) maps
\(qL^2N\) into \(pL^2N\), and \(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\) because
\(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\).  Thus \(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\).  Similarly
\(a p a^*\le q\), since \(a\in Q\ominus B\).  Centrality gives
\[
        2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q),
\]
so \(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power is an alternating
reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, hence \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  Therefore, for \(m\ne n\),
\[
 (h^me_Bh^{-m})(h^ne_Bh^{-n})
   =h^m e_BE_B(h^{\,n-m})e_Bh^{-n}=0 .
\]
The projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and have the same
\(\varphi\)-value, so that value is \(0\).  Hence
\(\varphi(1)=\varphi(e_B+p+q)=0\), a contradiction.  By DKEP Theorem 6.2,
\(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\), put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\), and use the standard graph-product
amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{6.1}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose
trace-zero unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If
\(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) give
\(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both
cases graph-product reduced-word calculus gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (6.1).  Proposition
\ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex lies in its own link,
\(\cap_iL_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives the
palindromic product of the \(q_{L_{v_i}}\)'s in
\(q_{\emptyset}\cA_Mq_{\emptyset}\).  But \(M_\emptyset=\C\), so
\(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor, hence non-amenable; separability follows from the finite graph and the separability of the
vertices.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the finite family
\(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.
```

```latex
\section*{Problem interpretation}

The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended
``irreducible'' hypothesis is join-irreducibility: there is no nonempty proper
partition $V=S\sqcup T$ such that every vertex of $S$ is adjacent to every
vertex of $T$.  Equivalently the complement graph $\Gamma^c$ is connected.
All vertex traces are faithful normal traces.  A trace-zero unitary in each
$M_v$ implies each vertex algebra is nontrivial and provides centered unitaries
for reduced-word and amalgamated-free-product arguments.

For $U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical expectation.  If $B\subset M$, $X_B$ is DKEP's
boundary piece generated by the Jones projection $e_B$.

\section*{DKEP normal-bidual notation}

Let
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
be DKEP's normal bidual.  For a boundary piece $X$, let $q_X$ be the support of
the normal corner $(\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*$ inside $\mathcal A_N$.  The
projection $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and with $JNJ$, and
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=
\{T\in\mathcal A_N:[T,a]\in q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X
\text{ for every }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and $\widetilde S(N)$.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to the trivial group action when $N$ is a separable
finite factor: $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X$ iff there is no
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

Important caution: a state on a von Neumann algebra which is $1$ on two
noncommuting projections need not be $1$ on their lattice meet, because the GNS
representation of a non-normal state need not preserve meets.  Thus the naive
criterion ``relative to finitely many $X_i$ plus
$\bigwedge_iq_{X_i}=q_0$'' is not justified unless commutation/normality is
available.  The replacement is to use an actual positive palindromic product of
the $q_i$'s which lies in the compact corner.

\section*{Palindromic finite-boundary criterion}

Let $N$ be a separable nonamenable finite factor and $X_1,\dots,X_m$ boundary
pieces.  Put $q_i=q_{X_i}$.  Suppose
\[
  R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Then relative proper proximality with respect to every $X_i$ implies ordinary
proper proximality.

Proof details:
if $\omega$ is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ with $\omega|_N=\tau$,
then for each $i$,
\[
  q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q_i^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
because $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$ and $[T,JNJ]\subset q_i\mathcal A_Nq_i$.
If $\omega(q_i^\perp)>0$, compression by $q_i^\perp$ gives an $N$-central state
on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ normal on $N$ (normality is by domination by a scalar
multiple of $\tau$ on $N_+$), contradiction.  Hence the GNS cyclic vector for
$\omega$ is fixed by each $q_i$ and therefore by $R$.  Since $R$ is a positive
contraction in $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, $1=\omega(R)\le \omega(q_0)$, so
$\omega(q_0)=1$.  Restricting $\omega$ to $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong
\mathbb B(L^2N)$ gives a Connes hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.

For graph products write $q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}$.  The needed replacement for a meet

  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.

This implies by induction that the palindromic product associated with
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ lies in the corner for $\cap_iU_i$.

Reasoning.  Let $\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$ with cyclic vector
$\zeta_U=1\otimes 1$.  The coefficient of $\zeta_U$ is $e_{M_U}$ and translates
of this vector generate DKEP's $X_{M_U}$.  DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ shows that products of a $W$-coefficient, a
$U$-coefficient, and a $W$-coefficient are coefficients of
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M \mathcal H_U\otimes_M \mathcal H_W
\]
(up to hereditary closure; the cyclic coefficient is
$e_{M_W}e_{M_U}e_{M_W}$).  Thus the normal corner on the left is contained in
the coefficient support of this triple fusion.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this triple fusion as a direct sum of
$L^2(M)\otimes_{M_Z}L^2(M)$ with $Z\subset U\cap W$.  Since $X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_{U\cap W}}$, the whole coefficient boundary is contained in
$X_{M_{U\cap W}}$.

This is intentionally weaker than the unsupported identity
$q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}$ and is tailored to non-normal states.

\section*{Relative Bass--Serre paradox}

For $N=P*_BQ$, $Q=B\bar\otimes A$, with $A$ containing a trace-zero unitary $a$
and with unitaries $w_1,w_2\in P$ satisfying
\[
  E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0,
\]
one proves $N$ properly proximal relative to $X_B$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the free-product Fock subspaces whose
first letter is in $L^2(P)\ominus L^2B$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2B$, respectively.
Then $1=e_B+p+q$.

Commutator formulas with right multiplication $R_x=Jx^*J$:
for $x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
  [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
  [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p,q$.  These formulas are checked directly on
reduced words; only the $B$-summand and one-letter words from the same free
component can change first-letter type.  Since right translates of $e_B$ are in
the boundary ideal, DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

If $\varphi$ is an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$, then
\[
  w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q.
\]
The first inequality uses centeredness of $w_i$ and orthogonality from
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; the second uses $a\in Q\ominus B$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so $\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.
The unitary $h=w_1a$ is $B$-Haar: the subspaces $h^nL^2B$ are pairwise
orthogonal.  Hence centrality forces $\varphi(e_B)=0$, contradicting
$1=e_B+p+q$.

\section*{Graph-product application}

For each vertex $v$,
\[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim v\}$.  Complement connectedness gives
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ has two vertices $r,t$, use trace-zero unitaries

connectedness and $|V|\ge3$ give $s\in\Lk(v)$ with $r\not\sim s$; set

properly proximal relative to every $X_{M_{\Lk(v)}}$.

The intersection of all links is empty.  The palindromic product of the link
boundary supports is therefore in the compact corner because
$M_\emptyset=\mathbb C$ and $X_{M_\emptyset}$ is the compact boundary piece.

CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives factoriality.  In a connected complement graph
with at least three vertices, there is no universal vertex and no isolated
exceptional nonedge component.  CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives nonamenability:
if $M$ were amenable, applying the relative amenability criterion with
$V_1=V,V_2=\emptyset$ would force every complement edge to be an isolated
exceptional edge, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least

Since $Z\subset I$ implies $e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_I}$ and $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}$,

\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A q_{U\cap W}.

$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending the
trace.  This is a Connes hypertrace, forcing amenability of the finite factor.

The critic objected that the previous support-sandwich argument multiplied

\section*{Refined normal-left-ideal support mechanism}

For an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\mathcal H\), DKEP's coefficient boundary
\(X_{\mathcal H}\) is generated hereditarily by the operators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\) bounded.  The most economical support object is
not the concrete \(C^*\)-algebra product
\(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}

\(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).  Its
right support in \(\mathcal A_N=(B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) is
\(q_{\mathcal H}\).  This follows because \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 identifies the
corresponding relative compact operators with the operators whose positive
squares lie in the relevant \(\mathbb K_X\)-corners.

Composition of rectangular normal bimodules is separately weak-* continuous in
the DKEP normal-bidual setting and agrees on bounded vectors with the concrete
```
